perturbation.original_text: PAYMENT TERMS: Payment shall be made upon receipt of proper invoice, Net (30) days (Reference Section 13.)
perturbation.changed_text: PAYMENT TERMS: Payment shall be made upon receipt of proper invoice, Net (90) days (Reference Section 13.)
perturbation.explanation: The original contract states payment terms of Net 30 days. By changing this to Net 90 days, the contract introduces a clause that could violate the Prompt Payment Act, which encourages federal agencies to pay contractors within 30 days. While this is a subcontract, delays in payment to the subcontractor could impact the prime contract's performance and potentially violate the spirit of the Prompt Payment Act if delays are unreasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Prompt Payment Act, 31 U.S. Code § 3903
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act generally requires federal agencies to pay invoices within 30 days. While this is a subcontract and not directly subject to the Act, a 'Net 90 days' payment term unreasonably delays payment and could violate the spirit of the Prompt Payment Act if it leads to delays in the prime contract's performance.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term from 'Net (30) days' to 'Net (90) days'. The Law Explanation suggests that this could violate the Prompt Payment Act, which encourages payment within 30 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 31 USC 3903, indicates regulations exist to carry out section 3902, with subsection (B) stating a 30-day payment window after a proper invoice is received. Although this subcontract might not be directly subject to the Act, the extension to 90 days is a clear departure from the 30-day timeframe encouraged by the Act, creating an inconsistency with its underlying principles and potentially impacting the prime contract, constituting a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Failure of the parties to reach agreement on any request for equitable adjustment, claim, appeal or action arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be a dispute to be resolved in accordance with the clause at FAR 52.233-1 Disputes, which is incorporated herein by reference.
perturbation.changed_text: Failure of the parties to reach agreement on any request for equitable adjustment, claim, appeal or action arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be a dispute to be resolved at the sole discretion of the BUYER. The BUYER's decision shall be final and binding.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the standard 'Disputes' clause (FAR 52.233-1) and replaces it with a clause giving the BUYER sole discretion in resolving disputes, making their decision final and binding. This eliminates the SELLER's right to appeal or pursue other legal remedies, which is a violation of standard contract law principles and the Contract Disputes Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text eliminates the SELLER's right to an impartial resolution of disputes, which is protected by the Contract Disputes Act. Specifically, 41 U.S.C. § 7104 outlines the procedures for contractor claims, including the right to appeal decisions to the Court of Federal Claims or an agency board of contract appeals. By stating that the BUYER's decision is 'final and binding,' the contract attempts to waive these statutory rights.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 48 CFR § 52.233-1 - Disputes. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 48 CFR § 52.233-1 - Disputes. CFR prev | next 52.233-1 Disputes . As prescribed in 33.215 , insert the following clause: Disputes (MAY 2014) (a) This contract is subject to 41 U.S.C. chapter 71 , Contract Disputes . (b) Except as provided in 41 U.S.C. chapter 71 , all disputes arising under or relating to this contract shall be resolved under this clause. (c) Claim, as used in this clause, means a written demand or written assertion by one of the contracting parties seeking, as a matter of right, the payment of money in a sum certain, the adjustment or interpretation of contract terms, or other relief arising under or relating to this contract . However, a written demand or written assertion by the Contractor seeking the payment of money exceeding $100,000 is not a claim under 41 U.S.C. chapter 71 until certified. A voucher, invoice , or other routine request for payment that is not in dispute when submitted is not a claim under 41 U.S.C. chapter 71 . The submission may be converted to a claim under 41 U.S.C. chapter 71 , by complying with the submission and certification requirements of this clause, if it is disputed either as to liability or amount or is not acted upon in a reasonable time. (d) (1) A claim by the Contractor shall be made in writing and, unless otherwise stated in this contract , submitted within 6 years after accrual of the claim to the Contracting Officer for a written decision. A claim by the Government against the Contractor shall be subject to a written decision by the Contracting Officer . (d) (2) (i) The Contractor shall provide the certification specified in paragraph (d)(2)(iii) of this clause when submitting any claim exceeding $100,000. (ii) The certification requirement does not apply to issues in controversy that have not been submitted as all or part of a claim . (iii) The certification shall state as follows: “I certify that the claim is made in good faith; that the supporting data are accurate and complete to the best of my knowledge and belief; that the amount requested accurately reflects the contract adjustment for which the Contractor believes the Government is liable; and that I am authorized to cer
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts the Contract Disputes Act. The original text incorporates FAR 52.233-1, which subjects the contract to 41 U.S.C. chapter 71. The changed text replaces this with a clause stating the BUYER's decision is 'final and binding,' eliminating the SELLER's right to appeal. This contradicts 41 U.S.C. § 7104, which states the 'Contractor's right of appeal from decision by contracting officer'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly mentions that a contractor has the right to appeal a contracting officer's decision within 90 days. By making the BUYER's decision final, the modified contract removes this right, creating a direct legal contradiction and non-compliance with the Contract Disputes Act.

perturbation.original_text: If the default condition is not remedied within ten (10) calendar days from the day of receipt of such letter, the issuer of the default termination letter may the without the necessity of any further notice, discontinue performance and terminate this Agreement for default and pursue any other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: If the default condition is not remedied within five (5) calendar days from the day of receipt of such letter, the issuer of the default termination letter may the without the necessity of any further notice, discontinue performance and terminate this Agreement for default and pursue any other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: This modification shortens the cure period for a default from ten calendar days to five. While not directly violating a specific federal statute, such a short cure period may be deemed unreasonable under general contract law principles, particularly if the default involves complex issues or requires significant corrective action. Reasonableness of cure periods is often determined on a case-by-case basis, but five days is arguably insufficient in many commercial contexts and could be challenged in court.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonableness of Notice and Cure Periods
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the cure period to 5 days, which may be considered commercially unreasonable. While not a direct violation of statute, reducing the cure period violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing that is present in every contract under UCC § 1-304. The reasonableness of the cure period is determined by looking at the nature of the contract, course of dealing, and trade usage.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: and Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act in 1906; the Uniform Bills of Lading Act and Uniform Stock Transfer Act in 1909; and the Uniform Conditional Sales Act in 1918. The ULC officially took on the task of drafting a comprehensive code to provide guidelines for all commercial transactions in 1940. In 1942, the ULC and the American Law Institute joined in a partnership that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the cure period from ten to five days, which, according to the Perturbation and Law Explanations, may be deemed commercially unreasonable and violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing UCC § 1-304, states that every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. While the snippet doesn't define 'good faith,' the reduction in the cure period could be interpreted as a breach of this obligation, potentially making the contract unenforceable, as the shorter period may not allow sufficient time to remedy the default, thus contradicting the implied duty of good faith.
